text: The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, moving it away from the pelvis to prevent obstruction during the laparoscopic myomectomy on a large posterior wall uterine fibroid.

The surgeon opens the space between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to access the uterine artery at its origin. The uterine artery is identified as the only structure crossing from lateral to medial in the pararectal space. It is carefully separated from the ureter below, and the surrounding fascia is dissected to isolate the artery. The isolated uterine artery is visible, with options to clip or coagulate it, while the ureter remains visibly distant from the artery.
The surgeon performs a dissection on the opposite side to identify the uterine artery. The ureter is visualized, and the uterine artery is coagulated on this side as well.

This significantly reduces the vascularity inside the fibroid and makes the surgery relatively bloodless. At our institute we prefer not to use vasopressin for any patient undergoing fibroid surgery irrespective of the size of the fibroid or the number of fibroids. We are a large volume center for performing fibroid surgery and we have found the technique uterine artery ligation at its origin or coagulation to be extremely effective in reducing the blood loss in patients undergoing laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon makes an incision in the uterus and inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid to provide traction and counter traction, facilitating the enucleation of the deep intramural fibroid from its bed. The fibroid is completely separated, revealing the intact endometrium cavity.
The surgeon closes the myoma bed using multilayered suturing with barbed sutures, maintaining a bloodless surgical field despite not using vasopressin.

The surgeon uses barbed sutures in a baseball manner to close the superficial layer of the myoma bed, ensuring the barbs are not exposed to the peritoneal cavity. The deeper layer is closed with continuous sutures, maintaining a bloodless field.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the uterine incision with minimal blood loss, using barbed sutures to close the opening of the uterus.
